question_text: Why does a piece of wood float on water but a piece of iron sinks?
correct_answer: Wood has a lower density than water, so it floats. Iron has a higher density than water, so it sinks.
explanation: Objects float when their density is less than the fluid they're in, and sink when their density is greater. Wood is less dense than water (about 0.6 g/cm³ compared to 1 g/cm³), while iron is much denser (about 7.9 g/cm³), so they behave very differently in water.
